hero_description: Developer passionate about building what drives technology. From developing resilient backend systems to programming gameplay and graphics, my goal is to create solutions that are robust behind the scenes and smooth on screen.
about_title: Turning Lines of Code Into Solutions
about_desc1: More than just writing code, my interest lies in creating efficient solutions. My background in Information Systems gave me a strong analytical foundation, which I apply at every stage of development, from architecture planning to implementation.
about_desc2: Whether developing movement logic and systems for an isometric game in Unity, or structuring the routes of an API with Spring Boot, I work with the same level of dedication. I'm always looking to expand my toolkit to become an increasingly versatile developer.
skill_tip_csharp: Proficient in the language for both Godot and Unity development
skill_tip_java: REST APIs with Java Spring Boot
skill_tip_sql: Database design, optimization, and query building
resume_summary_text: Early-career developer driven by curiosity to build systems from scratch. I have hands-on experience with personal projects in Game Dev (focused on logic and mechanics in Unity and Godot) and Backend (building services and APIs with Spring Boot and FastAPI). I highly value continuous learning, logical problem-solving, and constantly striving to write clean, organized code.
job1_bullet1: Development of gameplay mechanics and system architecture using Unity and Godot.
job1_bullet2: Programming focused on C#, implementing AI logic, physics, and state control.
job1_bullet3: Collaborative work in multidisciplinary teams for the ideation, prototyping, and delivery of interactive projects.
job2_bullet1: Support in structuring and maintaining API integrations and consumption.
job2_bullet2: Assisted in troubleshooting technical issues and understanding business rules for scalable solutions.
edu1_desc: Focus on software engineering and business insight.
edu2_desc: First steps in software development and fundamental concepts of database modeling and querying.
form_sent: Your message has been sent. Thank you!
dino_desc1: This project was developed for the Computer Organization and Architecture course and represents a deep dive into how hardware works internally. The goal was to recreate the classic "Dinosaur Game" using pure Assembly.
dino_desc2: Unlike traditional high-level development, this project required building all the physics logic, input handling, and the Game Loop while working directly with machine instructions and registers. The code was designed to run specifically on a custom processor simulator, requiring full adaptation to the environment's memory constraints and instruction set (ISA). Besides the low-level programming, I was also responsible for drawing the game's simplified pixel art, adapting visual references from the internet to fit within the simulator's strict graphical limitations.
dino_feat1_desc: 100% development in Assembly, working directly with machine instructions, register manipulation, and manual memory allocation to run the game's logic.
dino_feat2_desc: Code structured and optimized to run exclusively on a custom processor simulator, respecting the constraints and specific architecture of the academic environment.
dino_feat3_desc: Implementation of the game's continuous loop, keyboard input detection, and collision/jump calculations, all built from scratch without the help of any external library or engine.
dino_feat4_desc: Creation of visual assets (sprites) drawn pixel by pixel, adapting classic references to work around the simulator's severe graphical and rendering limitations.
arrow_desc1: Arrow Escape was my very first hands-on contact with Unity. I originally built the base of this project for the Fellowship of the Game (FoG) selection process. I recently decided to revisit it to apply the technical growth I've gained since then, turning the initial prototype into a finished, well-structured game.
arrow_desc2: In this precision 2D platformer, the player controls a charismatic marshmallow who must calculate jumps and dodge traps to complete the levels. The focus of this second version was refactoring the codebase, finishing the level design, and applying a new visual identity to the project.
arrow_feat1_desc: First hands-on implementation of a State Machine to control the player. Replaced complex conditional logic with a modular system, cleanly separating the jump, run, and fall states.
arrow_feat2_desc: Built and polished the scenarios using Unity's Tileset tools. Integrated free community assets to raise the visual quality and atmosphere of the levels.
arrow_feat3_desc: Revisited code built early in my journey to apply new Software Engineering knowledge, turning tightly coupled scripts into a clean, scalable architecture.
arrow_feat4_desc: Fine-tuned Rigidbody2D physics and collision detection to create responsive controls, essential for dodging traps and calculating precise jumps.
chaos_desc1: Chaos Survival holds a special place in my portfolio: it was the first project I built entirely on my own, from scratch, using Unity. The main goal here wasn't to create hyper-complex systems, but to master a game's full lifecycle (the Game Loop), understanding hands-on how the engine's pieces connect when you're the only one responsible for the code.
chaos_desc2: The game is a Top-Down Shooter focused on arena survival. To go beyond the basic 'walk and shoot,' the main technical challenge I set for myself was creating a dynamic item system. During a match, the player can pick up modifiers that change the gameplay rules — altering character stats or projectile behavior. This forced me to structure code flexible enough to receive these real-time status changes without breaking the core combat logic.
chaos_feat1_desc: Implemented independent controls, managing keyboard-based character movement simultaneously with mouse-driven aiming and shooting.
chaos_feat2_desc: Structured the full game cycle: dynamic enemy Spawner systems, Score tracking, Game Over conditions, and automatic scene restart.
chaos_feat3_desc: An individual hands-on lab focused on consolidating mastery of the engine's core concepts, such as Prefab handling, Rigidbody2D, Trigger-based collisions, and basic UI.
limpeza_desc1: The Urban Cleaning Management project was built to consolidate knowledge of relational modeling and data persistence in a professional environment. The application uses the Java Spring ecosystem on the backend to manage business logic and core CRUD operations on a PostgreSQL database.
limpeza_desc2: The main focus was building an organized, efficient system capable of handling structured queries and critical data persistence for the urban cleaning domain. To ensure portability and easy setup on any machine, the infrastructure was fully orchestrated with Docker Compose, integrating the database server, the management interface (PgAdmin), and the application into a single automated workflow.
limpeza_feat1_desc: Implemented a robust relational structure in PostgreSQL, focused on data normalization, referential integrity, and optimized SQL queries for the project's domain.
limpeza_feat2_desc: Built a Java Spring interface to orchestrate CRUD operations, applying good programming practices to ensure smooth communication between the code and the database.
limpeza_feat3_desc: Used Docker and Docker Compose to isolate and orchestrate the application and database. This approach ensures the system behaves identically across any development environment.
limpeza_feat4_desc: Applied business rules specific to urban management, organizing entities and processes that simulate real-world challenges in maintaining and organizing municipal services.
fragil_desc1: Built as a hands-on project for the Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) course, this game is a tile-based puzzle inspired by Club Penguin's nostalgic 'Thin Ice' minigame. The mechanical premise is simple but challenging: the player controls a penguin that must find a path to the exit, but each ice tile stepped on melts immediately, preventing any backtracking and demanding strategic planning.
fragil_desc2: Built entirely from scratch in Java, the project was an excellent lab for applying the pillars of Object Orientation to interactive systems development. To deepen the level design, I expanded the original concept by introducing different collectible item mechanics that trigger special effects and add new layers of complexity to solving the levels. The visuals were built by blending original pixel art of my own creation with adaptations of classic references, ensuring the puzzle's charm and visual clarity.
fragil_feat1_desc: 100% development in Java, applying Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Encapsulation concepts to structure the game's entities (player, tiles, and items) in a modular, clean way.
fragil_feat2_desc: Implemented a tile-based (matrix) movement math system, handling dynamic floor state updates (melting) and win/loss condition checks.
fragil_feat3_desc: Created an expandable architecture for collectible items, allowing objects picked up on the map to trigger special effects that change the puzzle's rules in real time.
fragil_feat4_desc: Built the visual rendering engine in Java itself, blending original assets I created with adapted resources from the internet to bring the interface and characters to life.
portal_desc1: Portal Shift was developed as part of my training and onboarding process at the Fellowship of the Game (FoG). The project's core goal wasn't to build a massive scope, but to solidify Unity fundamentals, C# code structuring, and a deep understanding of component and GameObject management.
portal_desc2: The core mechanic we built explores control modularity. The game doesn't have a single monolithic movement script; instead, we implemented a system where different movement scripts are dynamically enabled and disabled. As the player progresses through the level and interacts with delimited areas, the system reads these collisions and swaps the character's current script, fundamentally changing how physics and inputs respond in that specific scenario.
portal_feat1_desc: Implemented multiple modular control scripts, changing physics and input response in real time during gameplay.
portal_feat2_desc: Practiced clean architecture in Unity, handling GetComponent calls and the object lifecycle (Enable/Disable) to swap mechanics without destroying the main object.
sim_desc1: The Operating System Elements Simulator is an educational and testing platform built to demonstrate the practical behavior of classic Operating Systems concepts. With the frontend developed in React by other team members, my responsibility was to architect and build the entire engine behind the simulator, focusing on security, scalability, and low-level communication.
sim_desc2: The backend architecture was built in Java using Spring Boot. The big technical challenge was creating a system capable of receiving commands from the interface and running native C algorithms in isolation. To do this, I configured and integrated the backend with Docker, enabling the safe execution of rigorous tests, such as CPU-Bound and IO-Bound process simulations inside containers.
sim_feat1_desc: Configured and integrated the backend with Docker to run native C code in isolation, ensuring a safe sandbox environment for running processes without compromising the host.
sim_feat2_desc: Implemented bidirectional WebSocket communication for simulating the classic Producer-Consumer problem, continuously streaming execution results to the frontend (React).
sim_feat3_desc: Developed and controlled classic Operating Systems tests. The API can trigger and monitor C algorithms that demonstrate various general Operating Systems concepts.
sim_feat4_desc: Built a robust Java server that acts as the system's brain, seamlessly orchestrating communication between the user's web requests and the low-level execution.
